---
title: "10. Declaring and Diagnosing a Research Design"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb03_anatomy_of_design_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** Whether your research design is strong enough to run,
> decided by simulating it before you collect a single data point, reading how
> often it lands on the truth, and naming the one change that most improves it.

## Why this decision matters

Picture a methodologist on your committee reading your plan. She asks one
question: *"How often would this exact design catch the real effect if you ran it
a thousand times?"* If your answer is "I never checked," she stops there. A design
can read beautifully, with a sharp question and a clean comparison, and still come
back "nothing here" nine times out of ten, or land confidently on the wrong
number every time. This chapter lets you find that out cheaply, in simulation, so
a semester you would have spent collecting doomed data goes instead to a design
that can actually answer your question.

## The concept

In chapter 9 you built the four parts of a design. This chapter puts them to the
test with a three-step loop the course borrows from its RDSS text (*Research
Design in the Social Sciences: Declare, Diagnose, Redesign*).

**Declare** means writing your four design parts as something a computer can
run: a model that generates fake data, an inquiry it can compute on that fake
data, a plan for sampling and assigning conditions, and a rule for turning data
into an estimate. *Example:* code that invents 40 users, flips a coin to give each
a new interface, and subtracts the two group averages.

**Diagnose** means running that declared design many times on fresh simulated
worlds and watching how its estimate behaves. *Example:* run the coin-flip study
2,000 times and collect all 2,000 estimates. Three numbers summarize the pile.

**Bias** is the average distance between your estimate and the truth across the
runs; a design is unbiased when its estimates center on the true answer.
*Example:* if the truth is 2 and your estimates average 2.0, bias is about zero.
Bias is a systematic tilt, and more data never shrinks it.

**Variance** is how much the estimate wobbles from one run to the next. *Example:*
estimates swinging between -5 and +9 around a true value of 2 have high variance.
More data *does* shrink this.

**Power** is how often the design detects a real effect. *Example:* a design that
finds the effect in 8 of 100 runs has 8% power, which is almost useless.

**Redesign** means changing exactly one part in response to the diagnosis, then
diagnosing again. Low power from high variance calls for more data. Bias calls for
a different design, because size buys steadiness, not truth.

## A worked example

You are a **human-computer interaction** researcher asking whether an *inline
validation hint*, a small message that appears as you type ("password needs a
number") instead of only after you submit, helps people finish a signup form
faster. Your outcome is seconds-to-complete. Your true effect, in the world you
simulate, is that the hint shaves 8 seconds off.

You **declare** it: build users whose baseline times vary a lot, because some read
slowly and some race through; flip a coin to assign the hint version; subtract the
two group averages. You **diagnose** a pilot of 12 users per version. The result
is sobering. Bias is near zero, so the design aims true, but the spread is huge
because per-person times vary so much, and power comes back under 10%. The design
is honest and useless at once: it points at the 8-second gain on average and
almost never gets close enough on any single run to prove it.

Now **redesign**. Variance is the problem, so you raise the sample to 400 per
version and diagnose again. Power jumps past 90% and the spread collapses toward
the truth. That is variance cured by size. But run one more design as a warning:
drop the coin flip and let tech-savvy users self-select into the new version.
Savvy users finish faster anyway, so the hint gets credit for speed that
savviness delivered. This confounded design is just as tight at 400 per version,
yet it centers on a 14-second gain instead of 8, and no amount of extra data moves
it. That gap names the last idea. A randomized design can **identify** the hint's
effect, meaning pin the time gap on the hint and rule out other causes, while the
confounded one shows only an **association**, where the two move together but a
common cause could explain it.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb03 — The anatomy of a research design: MIDA +
declare → diagnose → redesign**. Open it in Colab with the
badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. In the lab you declare the
mentoring design in a few lines of Python, run it thousands of times to read its
bias, variance, and power with your own eyes, then watch one redesign rescue a
fair-but-powerless study while a confounded twin stays wrong at any sample size.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own expectation first, then delegate. Each prompt below leaves you a
draft you still have to check.

```text
Act as a simulation assistant. Here is my declared design in words:
[model, inquiry, data strategy, answer strategy]. Write the smallest Python
that builds a world where I set the true effect, runs the design many times,
and reports bias, variance, and power. List every assumption your code makes
about my design that I did not state.
```
After running, verify (counters *plausible-but-wrong-method*): re-run it with the
true effect set to zero and confirm it "detects" an effect only about as often as
your significance threshold allows. A simulation that fires on a zero effect is
declaring a different design than yours.

```text
Here is my diagnosed design and its numbers: [bias, variance, power]. List the
threats to any conclusion from one run as a table: threat, whether it is a
bias / variance / power problem, and the single redesign that reduces it most.
```
After running, verify (counters *illusion of completeness*): check the table
against your own printed diagnosis. The killer your numbers already proved must be
the top row; a tidy six-threat list that never names it missed the point.

```text
Argue against my claim that this design is strong enough to run. As a hostile
methodologist, name the one diagnosand I am most likely fooling myself about,
and the observation that would expose it.
```
After running, verify (counters *sycophantic agreement*): if it praises the design
without a single objection, discard the answer and diagnose again yourself.

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
Three calls stay yours. **Which world your question assumes**, the model you
declare, is a claim about reality no tool can make for you. **Which single quantity
you name as your inquiry** decides what "success" even means. And **the honest call
of whether to run a design your own diagnosis says is too weak** is a judgment you
sign your name to. An AI can propose threats and draft simulation code, but it
cannot decide that your under-powered study is worth a semester anyway.
:::

## An AI failure case

You paste your confounded observational design and ask, "My estimate is noisy.
What should I do?" The tool answers with confidence: "Recruit more participants to
tighten your estimate." That sounds obviously right and is exactly wrong for your
case. Your problem is **bias** from self-selection, not variance, and more data
only makes a biased estimate a more *precise* wrong number. You catch it the way
this chapter teaches: run the confounded design in simulation at a large sample
and watch the estimates cluster tightly around 14 seconds when the truth is 8. The
spread shrank; the error did not. That is the `plausible-but-wrong-method`
failure, and your own diagnosis unmasks it.

## Verification laboratory

The method here is **simulation** (verification guide, method 2: reach for it when
a claim is about how a procedure behaves, not a fixed number). Never trust an AI's
"your design has about 80% power." Declare the design yourself, set a true effect
you choose, run it a few thousand times, and read the power off your own pile of
estimates. For any design that claims a *cause*, add an independent second check:
**causal diagram** (method 8). Draw your model as a DAG and look for a common cause
with an arrow into both your treatment and your outcome. If that backdoor is open,
no power number rescues you, and your honest claim stops at "associated with."

## Project transfer

This chapter carries you through the back half of **M3, your research charter and
MIDA declaration**, the three-minute declaration you present at Friday's studio.
Chapter 9 gave you the four parts; here you declare them as a runnable design,
diagnose it for bias, variance, and power, and bring one named redesign. Your M3
is graded on drawing the identify-versus-associate line for your own design, so
the diagnosis you run this week is the evidence behind the boundary you will
defend.

## Defend your decision

In five sentences, declare and defend one design for your project. State the
single quantity you are after (your inquiry). Report the worst diagnosis number
you can estimate, bias, variance, or power, and say what it does to your answer.
Then make the honest call: run it, redesign first, or narrow the claim, and name
the one change that would most improve it. Close with the boundary: does your
design **identify** an effect or only show an **association**, and what remains
uncertain even if everything goes right?
